policy_statement: Hiroshima City will set a short-term (FY2020) energy-use reduction target of 5% for total energy use not affected by the CO2 emission factor of electric utilities.
mechanism_description: Hiroshima City will set a short-term target to reduce total energy use, leading to lower overall energy consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Human activity (energy demand) → ↓ energy production/combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Citywide actors (municipal operations, buildings, industry, households) are steered to reduce total final energy use through a quantified short-term reduction target. (2) Process: Lower energy demand leads to reduced combustion of fossil fuels and reduced upstream generation of electricity/heat (depending on the supply mix). (3) Outcome: Net reduction in CO2e emissions from energy use (direct and/or indirect), yielding climate mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing total energy use most directly lowers fossil energy consumption and associated CO2 (and other GHG) emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: setting an energy-use reduction target aims to cut total energy demand, which reduces fuel use/electricity generation and thereby reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “resource efficiency” describes using less energy for the same service, the climate-dominant effect of this mechanism (as written) is emissions reduction from reduced energy throughput; thus Mitigation is the correct primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If energy is already near-zero-carbon, the emissions impact could be smaller and the policy could look more like Resource Efficiency; however, the mechanism’s intent and typical effect across contexts is still to reduce energy-related emissions.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose mechanism is “energy use reduction target → energy use reduction” fundamentally operates by reducing aggregate energy consumption; the biggest climate-relevant consequence is reduced energy-related GHG emissions, so Mitigation applies broadly.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary subtype or sectoral attribution (buildings/industry/municipal ops; behavioral vs. efficiency) depends on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the reduced energy is entirely from zero-carbon supply (or reductions are primarily load-shifting without net energy reduction), mitigation benefits may be limited and “Resource Efficiency” could be argued; however the canonical mechanism specifies net energy-use reduction, which generally implies emissions reduction in most grids and fuel mixes.
key_indicators: "Setting of short-term energy reduction target"; "FY2020"; "Total amount of energy use"; "▲ 5%"; "not affected by the carbon dioxide emission factor of electric utility"
co_benefits: Reduced energy costs; improved local air quality; energy security/resilience
instance_edge_case_notes: “Not affected by the CO2 emission factor of electric utilities” suggests the target is defined to avoid variability from grid emission factors (i.e., focusing on final energy use), but the instrument remains a target rather than a performance standard or mandate because no compliance/enforcement mechanism is stated.
